extra.reasoning:
The property is the invariant opted_out ⟹ ¬sent over all reachable states. It holds by induction.

Initial state: opted_out=false, so the implication is vacuously true.

Preservation under each transition:
- t1 (optout): sets opted_out:=true AND sent:=false simultaneously. Post-state: opted_out=true, sent=false → invariant holds.
- t2 (send_attempt, guard: not opted_out): the only transition that sets sent:=true, but it requires opted_out=false. It does not modify opted_out, so post-state has opted_out=false → invariant vacuously holds.
- t3 (send_attempt, guard: opted_out): sets sent:=false → invariant holds regardless of opted_out.
- t4 (tick): sets sent:=false (and increments ticks) → invariant holds.

Key observations: (1) opted_out is monotonic — no transition ever resets it to false. (2) The only way sent becomes true is t2, which is disabled whenever opted_out is true. (3) Every transition reachable while opted_out is true (t1, t3, t4) sets sent:=false; t2 is disabled. Therefore no reachable state can have opted_out=true and sent=true. The invariant holds initially and is preserved by every transition, so it holds on every reachable execution (well within the 10-event bound). The property holds; no counterexample exists.
